channel.title: Science - Latest - Google News
channel.copyright: Copyright © 2026 Google. All rights reserved. This XML feed is made available solely for the purpose of rendering Google News results within a personal feed reader for personal, non-commercial use. Any other use of the feed is expressly prohibited. By accessing this feed or using these results in any manner whatsoever, you agree to be bound by the foregoing restrictions.

item.title: Interstellar comet may be oldest object seen in our solar system, research finds - The Guardian
item.description:
1. Interstellar comet may be oldest object seen in our solar system, research finds  The Guardian
  2. NASA’s Webb Finds Clues to Ancient, Distant Origin of Comet 3I/ATLAS  NASA Science (.gov)
  3. The Chemistry of Interstellar Comet 3I/ATLAS Is Unlike Anything We've Ever Seen  ScienceAlert
  4. Radio scans find no alien tech from the latest interstellar comet  Phys.org
  5. Gemini North Spots the Changing Comet 3I/ATLAS  NOIRLab

item.title: James Webb telescope detects 'galaxy-killing wind' near the dawn of time - Live Science
item.description:
1. James Webb telescope detects 'galaxy-killing wind' near the dawn of time  Live Science
  2. Revealing how and when a black hole's mighty winds can squash star formation  Phys.org
  3. ⭐ Why, at a certain mass, do galaxies stop forming stars?  Techno-Science.net
  4. Black hole winds may be robbing giant galaxies of their future stars  ScienceDaily
  5. Black Hole Winds Shut Down Star Formation: XRISM Measures Magnetic Trigger for First Time  Tech Times

item.title: NASA’s Nancy Grace Roman Space Telescope arrives in Florida - Spaceflight Now
item.description:
1. NASA’s Nancy Grace Roman Space Telescope arrives in Florida  Spaceflight Now

  4. NASA’s new revolutionary space telescope arrives at Kennedy Space Center  WFTV
  5. NASA’s Soon-to-Launch Telescope Is Expected to Unveil Distant Worlds, ‘Millions’ of Stars’ and ‘Billions of Galaxies’  People.com

item.title: SpaceX is launching a secret spacecraft that could change how things are made in space - Teslarati
item.description:

  4. Last Mile of Space and Earth Rocket Package Delivery  NextBigFuture.com
  5. SpaceX to launch uncrewed Starfall cargo capsule  Spectrum News 13

item.title: A space telescope that has spent 22 years detecting the most powerful explosions in the universe is now falling toward Earth, and a startup in Arizona built its rescue craft in seven months flat — a schedule Science described as almost unheard of for a NASA m - Space Daily
item.description:
1. A space telescope that has spent 22 years detecting the most powerful explosions in the universe is now falling toward Earth, and a startup in Arizona built its rescue craft in seven months flat — a schedule Science described as almost unheard of for a NASA m  Space Daily
  2. 'No one thought it was going to be possible.' A space telescope is falling out of space. This is NASA's daring plan to save it. (video)  Space
  3. Rocket Attached to Aircraft for Katalyst-NASA Swift Boost  NASA Science (.gov)
  4. A Never-Before-Attempted Space Telescope Rescue Is LaunchingThis Week  IFLScience
  5. A bold satellite rescue mission came together in record time, but will it work?  Ars Technica

item.title: For a decade the Pink Planet was too faint for any ground telescope to study — and when James Webb finally caught its spectrum, the best fit to the light was an atmosphere hazed with salt clouds - Space Daily
item.description:
1. For a decade the Pink Planet was too faint for any ground telescope to study — and when James Webb finally caught its spectrum, the best fit to the light was an atmosphere hazed with salt clouds  Space Daily
  2. "Pink Planet" is surrounded by salty clouds, researchers using Webb telescope find  CBS News
  3. James Webb Space Telescope finds a salty surprise on famous 'Pink Planet'  Space
  4. Webb telescope finds possible salt clouds on a pink alien world  Mashable
  5. JWST-TST High Contrast: First Direct Spectroscopy of GJ 504 b Reveals Clouds And Possible Metal Enrichment  astrobiology.com

item.title: An unusual strategy helps some tropical butterflies live 25 times longer than their relatives - KSL News
item.description:
1. An unusual strategy helps some tropical butterflies live 25 times longer than their relatives  KSL News

  3. Some tropical butterfly species have unlocked an evolutionary way to live longer  CNN
  4. The Butterfly Longevity Diet  The Atlantic
  5. Butterflies may hold clues to longer lives  NPR

item.title: Scientists Just Found Something Weird Inside Moss - SciTechDaily
item.description:

  4. Desert moss may hide one of Earth's oldest partnerships  Earth.com

item.title: Remember that star that was supposed to explode in 2024? It didn't, but it could still happen soon - CBC

item.title: A mysterious gamma-ray stream comes from the Milky Way's center. Could dark matter have something to do with it? - Space
item.description:
1. A mysterious gamma-ray stream comes from the Milky Way's center. Could dark matter have something to do with it?  Space
  2. Dark matter may be the source of mysterious radiation from the center of the galaxy  Universe Space Tech
  3. Dark matter cannot be ruled out as cause of gamma ray glow at the Milky Way's center, machine learning shows  Phys.org
  4. A strange glow in the heart of the Milky Way may be dark matter  The Brighter Side of News
  5. Dark Matter in the Center of the Milky Way Not Ruled Out | Newswise  Newswise

item.title: The most unpredictable meteor shower of the year peaks this week. Here's what to expect - Space
item.description:
1. The most unpredictable meteor shower of the year peaks this week. Here's what to expect  Space
  2. Meteor Activity Outlook for June 20-26, 2026  American Meteor Society
  3. The June Bootids have flared to dozens of meteors an hour in rare years like 1998, but most years the shower is so weak it shows almost nothing — and no outburst is forecast for 2026  Space Daily
  4. June Bootids Meteor Shower 2026: What, when, where and how to see "unpredictable" meteor shower?  Moneycontrol.com
  5. Get ready: A rare meteor shower could put on a spectacular surprise show next week  MSN

item.title: During the 1859 solar storm, telegraph operators disconnected their batteries entirely — yet messages kept flowing on the storm's own current as sparks showered from the machines and the paper caught fire - Space Daily
item.description: During the 1859 solar storm, telegraph operators disconnected their batteries entirely — yet messages kept flowing on the storm's own current as sparks showered from the machines and the paper caught fire  Space Daily

item.description:

  2. Our Hidden Fungal Networks Could Reach Beyond the Solar System  Mother Jones
  3. A Hidden Universe: Underground Fungi Could Stretch to the Sun Nearly a Billion Times  atmos.earth
  4. Nearly All Plants Depend on Tiny Underground Fungi. The Microbes' Vast Global Networks Were Just Mapped for the First Time  Smithsonian Magazine
  5. There’s an Unfathomably Huge Network of Fungi Lurking Beneath the Earth  VICE

item.title: Our galaxy and its neighbours are racing through space at more than two million kilometres an hour, caught in a tug-of-war between the gravity of distant superclusters ahead and a vast, near-empty void shoving us from behind - Space Daily
item.description: Our galaxy and its neighbours are racing through space at more than two million kilometres an hour, caught in a tug-of-war between the gravity of distant superclusters ahead and a vast, near-empty void shoving us from behind  Space Daily

item.title: Solar storms don't shock people directly — they induce electric currents in long metal, meaning power lines, pipelines and undersea internet cables become the very antennas that channel the damage into modern life - Space Daily
item.description: Solar storms don't shock people directly — they induce electric currents in long metal, meaning power lines, pipelines and undersea internet cables become the very antennas that channel the damage into modern life  Space Daily

item.title: Thawing Permafrost Not Only Emits CO2, It Absorbs it Too, Shows Landmark Study - Good News Network
item.description:

  3. Rock weathering can counteract river CO2 emissions induced by permafrost thaw  Nature
  4. Northern permafrost switches from carbon sink to carbon source earlier than thought in models including deep soil carbon  Phys.org

item.title: It's Official: Third Galaxy Found With No Dark Matter May Solve an Epic Mystery - ScienceAlert
item.description:

  4. In a puzzling chain: Another galaxy found completely without dark matter  heise online
  5. Astronomers atop Maunakea discover third galaxy without dark matter  Maui Now

item.title: On 5 March 1982, the Soviet Venera 14 lander reached the surface of Venus, ejected the protective cap from its camera lens, photographed the surrounding terrain — and then lowered its mechanical soil-testing arm directly onto the discarded lens cap, returni - 19FortyFive
item.description:
1. On 5 March 1982, the Soviet Venera 14 lander reached the surface of Venus, ejected the protective cap from its camera lens, photographed the surrounding terrain — and then lowered its mechanical soil-testing arm directly onto the discarded lens cap, returni  19FortyFive
  2. Venus’ Strange Rotation Was Likely Triggered By A High Velocity Moon-Sized Impactor  Universe Today

item.title: America's only landing on Venus was an accident — in 1978 NASA dropped four cone-shaped probes into the Venusian atmosphere, none built to survive hitting the ground, and one of them struck the surface, kept transmitting for 67 minutes and 37 seconds, - 19FortyFive

item.title: No spacecraft has ever landed in the outer solar system — except one: the Huygens probe, which parachuted through Titan's orange haze in 2005 and touched down more than a billion kilometres from Earth in cold that dropped below minus 170 degrees Cels - Space Daily
item.description: No spacecraft has ever landed in the outer solar system — except one: the Huygens probe, which parachuted through Titan's orange haze in 2005 and touched down more than a billion kilometres from Earth in cold that dropped below minus 170 degrees Cels  Space Daily

item.title: For decades the heavy rocks that carve trails across a Death Valley lakebed were never seen moving, until cameras caught them gliding on thin rafts of melting ice pushed by a light wind - Space Daily
item.description: For decades the heavy rocks that carve trails across a Death Valley lakebed were never seen moving, until cameras caught them gliding on thin rafts of melting ice pushed by a light wind  Space DailySee more headlines & perspectives on Google News

item.title: About 66 million years ago an asteroid roughly 10-15 km wide struck off Mexico's Yucatan, gouging a crater some 180 km across — and the extinction that followed wiped out the non-avian dinosaurs and around 75% of all species - Space Daily
item.description:
1. About 66 million years ago an asteroid roughly 10-15 km wide struck off Mexico's Yucatan, gouging a crater some 180 km across — and the extinction that followed wiped out the non-avian dinosaurs and around 75% of all species  Space Daily
  2. Were dinosaurs doomed before the asteroid hit?  Sciworthy

item.title: Apollo 12 was struck by lightning twice in the first 52 seconds of flight, filling the spacecraft with warning lights and turning Mission Control’s data into nonsense — until a young controller named John Aaron recognised an obscure failure pattern and calmly sai - Space Daily
item.description: Apollo 12 was struck by lightning twice in the first 52 seconds of flight, filling the spacecraft with warning lights and turning Mission Control’s data into nonsense — until a young controller named John Aaron recognised an obscure failure pattern and calmly sai  Space Daily

item.title: NASA spent 14 years keeping the Galileo spacecraft alive through a jammed antenna and brutal radiation, then in 2003 deliberately flew it into Jupiter to be destroyed — not because it had failed, but because it had found a probable ocean on Europa it could n - 19FortyFive
item.description: NASA spent 14 years keeping the Galileo spacecraft alive through a jammed antenna and brutal radiation, then in 2003 deliberately flew it into Jupiter to be destroyed — not because it had failed, but because it had found a probable ocean on Europa it could n  19FortyFive

item.title: Voyager 1 launched in 1977 on a four-year mission and is still flying 49 years later, now so far away that in November 2026 a radio signal will take a full 24 hours to reach it — so when engineers say "good morning" on a Monday, the answer won't arrive until - 19FortyFive
item.description:
1. Voyager 1 launched in 1977 on a four-year mission and is still flying 49 years later, now so far away that in November 2026 a radio signal will take a full 24 hours to reach it — so when engineers say "good morning" on a Monday, the answer won't arrive until  19FortyFive
  2. It's Official: Voyager 1 Will Break A Cosmic Record, Reaching 1 Light-Day From Earth On November 18, 2026  IFLScience
  3. A probe is about to enter a realm humanity has only imagined until now  Futura, le média qui explore le monde

item.title: In the weightlessness of orbit, an astronaut’s heart can become more spherical as it no longer works against gravity in the usual way, while their spine stretches enough to make them measurably taller before they return to Earth. - Space Daily
item.description: In the weightlessness of orbit, an astronaut’s heart can become more spherical as it no longer works against gravity in the usual way, while their spine stretches enough to make them measurably taller before they return to Earth.  Space Daily

item.description:

  2. The Milky Way’s huge black hole was missing a key feature. Scientists say they finally detected it  CNN
  3. The Milky Way’s black hole is eerily quiet. Scientists have now found evidence of its missing wind  CTV News

item.title: In December 2024 a NASA spacecraft flew straight into the Sun's outer atmosphere at 430,000 miles per hour, the fastest any human-made object has ever moved, through a region millions of degrees hot — and survived because a 4.5-inch carbon shield kept - 19FortyFive
item.description:
1. In December 2024 a NASA spacecraft flew straight into the Sun's outer atmosphere at 430,000 miles per hour, the fastest any human-made object has ever moved, through a region millions of degrees hot — and survived because a 4.5-inch carbon shield kept  19FortyFive
  2. NASA's Parker Solar Probe flew in and out of the solar corona and found a source of high-energy particles that no existing model had predicted  Space Daily
  3. A spacecraft travelling at Parker Solar Probe’s peak speed would reach the Moon in about 35 minutes — a trip that took Apollo 11 roughly three days  Space Daily

item.title: In September 2017, after 20 years in space, NASA deliberately flew the Cassini spacecraft into Saturn and let it burn up like a meteor — not because it had failed, but because it had found a possibly habitable ocean on the moon Enceladus that a dead spacecr - 19FortyFive
item.description: In September 2017, after 20 years in space, NASA deliberately flew the Cassini spacecraft into Saturn and let it burn up like a meteor — not because it had failed, but because it had found a possibly habitable ocean on the moon Enceladus that a dead spacecr  19FortyFive
